---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (геологический разрез скважины / каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite well log typical of Soviet-era mineral and hydrocarbon exploration. It appears to be a lithological-stratigraphic column combined with geophysical logging curves, presented in the standard vertical format used by Soviet geological survey organizations (ВСЕГЕИ/regional geological expeditions). The log runs vertically from top (shallow) to bottom (deep), with multiple parallel tracks or columns representing different parameters.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log covers a **single borehole (скважина)**, though the specific borehole number is not clearly legible at this resolution — it may be something like **скв-1** or a similar designation visible in the header. The log appears to cover a substantial depth interval, likely on the order of **several hundred to perhaps 1,000–1,500 metres** based on the physical length of the log sheet. The geographic location is northern Kazakhstan, consistent with the regional survey context (map sheets M-42 and N-42, 1:200,000 scale). The header region contains what appears to be a project/report identifier and location data, but precise values are largely **[illegible]** at this scan resolution.

## Key Content

The figure is organized into several vertical tracks/columns running the full depth of the borehole:

### Left Side — Lithological and Stratigraphic Columns:
- **Leftmost columns** appear to contain:
  - A **stratigraphic/age column** with formation names and possibly system/series symbols (Cyrillic abbreviations)
  - A **lithological description column** (словесное описание пород) with written rock descriptions
  - A **core/sample recovery column** or drilling parameters column
  - A **lithological symbol column** showing graphical patterns representing rock types (hatching patterns typical for sandstone, limestone, clay, crystalline basement, etc.)

- The leftmost margin appears to show **depth markers** at regular intervals (possibly every 10 or 20 metres), though specific values are **[illegible]**

### Central Tracks — Geophysical Logging Curves:
The central portion of the log shows **two or more geophysical curve tracks** with clearly visible oscillating/deflecting traces:

- **One prominent curve** (appearing as a bold, irregular waveform) likely represents **КС (кажущееся сопротивление / apparent resistivity)** or **ПС (потенциал самополяризации / spontaneous potential / SP curve)** — the two most standard curves in Soviet borehole logging
- A **second curve** running roughly parallel or adjacent, possibly representing a different electrode spacing or a **gamma-ray (ГК)** or **neutron log (НГК)** curve
- The curves show **significant deflections and anomalies** at multiple depth intervals, particularly:
  - A zone of **high-amplitude deflections** in the upper-to-middle portion of the log (roughly the upper third), suggesting lithological contrasts — possibly the transition from sedimentary cover into a more resistive or structurally complex interval
  - A **relatively calm / low-amplitude zone** in parts of the middle section
  - **Renewed anomalies** in the lower portion
  - The **bottommost section** (below what appears to be a break or separator line) shows a distinct set of curves, possibly representing a different logging run or a continuation below a casing point

### Right Side — Data Tables and Annotations:
- The **right-hand columns** contain tabular data, likely including:
  - Sample/interval numbers
  - Core recovery percentages
  - Laboratory analysis results (possibly chemical or mineralogical data)
  - Formation tops and depths
  - Drilling parameters
- These columns are largely **[illegible]** due to scan quality but appear to contain numerical data in a grid format

### Notable Structural Features:
- There appears to be a **horizontal break or separator** approximately two-thirds down the log, possibly indicating a casing point, a lost-circulation zone, or a logging run boundary
- The **uppermost section** shows a flat/quiet curve trace suggesting unconsolidated overburden or casing
- The **transition zone** where curves begin showing large deflections likely marks the top of consolidated or crystalline basement, or a significant lithological boundary — consistent with the survey's interest in basement structure

## Research Value

This composite borehole log provides **direct subsurface lithological and geophysical data** for a specific drillhole location within the northern Kazakhstan survey area, allowing correlation of surface geology and geophysical interpretations (gravity, magnetic, seismic profiles from the broader survey) with actual rock types and formation depths encountered in drilling.
